key_points: In vitro study comparing conventional irrigation (CI), passive ultrasonic irrigation (PUI), and Er:YAG laser-activated irrigation (LAI) in both conventional (CIA) and minimally invasive access (MIA) cavities using extracted maxillary first molars infected with E. faecalis.

Both PUI and LAI significantly reduced bacterial CFU counts compared to conventional irrigation alone (P < 0.05); no significant difference was found between PUI and LAI in root canal lumen disinfection.
In dentin debris analysis (after enlargement to F3), LAI (group C) showed the least bacterial CFU counts, suggesting superior penetration into dentinal tubules compared to PUI.

Minimally invasive access with conventional irrigation (group 2A) performed better than conventional access with conventional irrigation (group 1A), possibly due to the more focused irrigant flow in MIA.
No significant difference was found between CIA and MIA when using PUI or LAI, suggesting that activated irrigation techniques compensate for the access limitations of minimally invasive cavities.
Er:YAG LAI is described as easier to operate than ultrasonic in the context of minimally invasive root canal treatment.
Instrumentation was performed to ProTaper Universal F2 before disinfection; canals were then enlarged to F3 for dentin debris collection.
E. faecalis infection model was used; results may not fully represent polymicrobial clinical infections.

outcomes.measured: Bacterial CFU counts in root canal lumen after disinfection
outcomes.result: PUI and LAI both significantly reduced CFU vs. CI (P < 0.05); no significant difference between PUI and LAI (P > 0.05)
outcomes.clinical_significance: Both activated irrigation methods are more effective than conventional irrigation regardless of access type

outcomes.measured: Bacterial CFU counts in dentin debris after enlargement to F3
outcomes.result: LAI (group C) had the least CFU counts; significant antagonism between access type and disinfection method (F = 7.224, P = 0.002)
outcomes.clinical_significance: Er:YAG LAI may penetrate dentinal tubules more effectively than PUI

outcomes.measured: Comparison of CIA vs. MIA with conventional irrigation
outcomes.result: MIA + CI (group 2A) was significantly better than CIA + CI (group 1A) (P < 0.05)
outcomes.clinical_significance: MIA geometry may concentrate irrigant flow, improving CI efficacy

outcomes.measured: Comparison of CIA vs. MIA with PUI or LAI
outcomes.result: No significant difference between group 1B and 2B, or group 1C and 2C (P > 0.05)
outcomes.clinical_significance: Activated irrigation techniques (PUI and LAI) overcome the disinfection limitations of minimally invasive access

notes: In vitro study using extracted teeth; single-species E. faecalis model limits clinical generalizability. Specific Er:YAG laser parameters (power, frequency, pulse duration, fiber type) are not fully detailed in the abstract.

population.condition: Extracted maxillary first molars infected with E. faecalis

superiority_claim: El autor afirma que 'Er:YAG laser is better than ultrasonic in removing bacteria from dentinal tubules and is easy to operate, which is more suitable for minimally invasive root canal treatment.'
source_citation: Shan, X., Tian, F., Li, J., Yang, N., Wang, Y., & Sun, H. (2022). Comparison of Er:YAG laser and ultrasonic in root canal disinfection under minimally invasive access cavity. Lasers in Medical Science. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10103-022-03613-0
clinical_indication: Root canal disinfection in minimally invasive and conventional access cavities
uncertainties: Specific Er:YAG laser parameters (power in W, frequency in Hz, pulse duration in ms/μs, fiber/tip type, water/air spray settings) are not reported in the abstract; full text may contain these details.
The mechanism by which MIA + CI outperforms CIA + CI is speculative (focused irrigant flow hypothesis); not experimentally confirmed.
Single-species E. faecalis model may not reflect clinical polymicrobial root canal infections.
No information on fiber tip type used for LAI (endofire, radial stripped, or other) — relevant for protocol replication.

protocols.name: Activación de irrigante con Er:YAG (LAI) en acceso mínimamente invasivo guiado por computadora
synonyms_es: irrigación asistida por láser Er:YAG endodóntico
irrigación láser en cavidad de acceso mínimo
protocols.purpose: Activar el irrigante endodóntico (NaOCl) mediante pulsos de Er:YAG 2940 nm dentro del conducto radicular para mejorar la desinfección tanto en el lumen del conducto como en los túbulos dentinarios, especialmente en cavidades de acceso mínimamente invasivo guiadas por computadora.

parameters_table.equipment_class: Cualquier Er:YAG 2940 nm con fibra intracanal compatible
parameters_table.fibra_punta: No especificado (fibra intracanal; tipo no reportado en el estudio)

critical_notes: Los parámetros específicos del láser Er:YAG (potencia, frecuencia, duración de pulso, tipo de fibra, spray agua/aire) no fueron reportados en el abstract del estudio; consultar el texto completo para detalles del protocolo.
El estudio es in vitro con modelo de E. faecalis en molares maxilares extraídos; la generalización clínica a infecciones polimicrobianas debe hacerse con cautela.

El documento presenta LAI favorablemente respecto a PUI para remoción de bacterias en túbulos dentinarios; existen alternativas (PUI, irrigación sónica, PIPS™) con mecanismos de acción diferentes que pueden ser apropiadas según el caso clínico.
La instrumentación se realizó hasta ProTaper Universal F2 antes de la desinfección; los conductos se ampliaron a F3 para la recolección de debris dentinario.
No se reportó diferencia significativa entre LAI y PUI en la desinfección del lumen del conducto; la ventaja de LAI se observó principalmente en el debris dentinario (túbulos).
technique_steps: Preparar el acceso endodóntico (convencional o mínimamente invasivo guiado por computadora) e instrumentar los conductos hasta ProTaper Universal F2.
Establecer el modelo de infección con E. faecalis en todos los conductos radiculares.
Introducir la fibra del láser Er:YAG 2940 nm en el conducto radicular.
Activar el irrigante (NaOCl) mediante pulsos de Er:YAG dentro del conducto.
Recolectar muestras microbianas con puntas de papel para cuantificación de UFC.
Ampliar los conductos a ProTaper Universal F3 y recolectar debris dentinario para análisis microbiológico adicional.
indications: Desinfección de conductos radiculares en acceso mínimamente invasivo guiado por computadora
Desinfección de conductos radiculares en acceso convencional
Casos donde se requiere mayor penetración del irrigante en túbulos dentinarios
Tratamiento de conductos infectados con E. faecalis (modelo in vitro)
contraindications: Uso sin irrigante activo en el conducto (el láser activa el irrigante, no reemplaza la irrigación)
Aplicación directa sobre tejidos periapicales sin control de longitud de trabajo
protocols.evidence_level: Bajo — estudio in vitro con modelo de E. faecalis en dientes extraídos; n=66 dientes divididos en 6 subgrupos; no hay seguimiento clínico.
